summary: Drew DeVault addresses the common question of how he manages to maintain so many FOSS projects by himself, answering simply: he doesn't do it alone. The post is essentially a public thank-you to the thousands of contributors who have made his projects successful. He presents a massive list of individual contributors by name, emphasizing that each is a real person who voluntarily gave their time. The post serves as both an acknowledgment of collective effort and a rebuttal to the myth of the solo open source maintainer.
key_points: The success of FOSS projects is never the work of a single person, even when one person is the visible maintainer
Thousands of distinct contributors have participated across DeVault's projects, each deserving individual recognition
Contributors are real people with their own lives who chose to invest time in shared software
Public attribution and gratitude are important responsibilities for project maintainers
money_quotes: I'm often asked how I can get so much done, being just one person. The answer is: I'm not just one person.
I have enjoyed the help of thousands of talented people who have contributed to these works. Without them, none of the projects I work on would be successful.
If you've enjoyed any of the projects I've worked on, you owe thanks to some of these wonderful people.
Each of these is a distinct person, with their own lives and aspirations, who took time out of those lives to help build some cool software.
I owe everything to these wonderful, talented, dedicated people.
Let's keep up the good work, together.
key_insight: The prolific open source maintainer is a myth — behind every successful project stands a community of contributors who deserve recognition.
